talk_metadata.session_title: Advancing Early Phase Pediatric Clinical Trials to Turn Promising Agents into Actionable Evidence
talk_metadata.talk_title: Menin Inhibitors: From IND to Pediatric Approval of Revumenib
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "we did this very quickly, four years from the first dosing to basically the NDA"
Speaker said: "we didn't see any difference in the PK in the younger patients"
Speaker said: "the single patient protocol allowed us to collect PK and some other safety parameters and even some efficacy data"

key_data_gaps: Limited patient numbers (single patient protocols and early phase cohort only)
No formal biomarker assay beyond blast count

crispro_opportunity.description: Revumenib development relied on blast count reduction without a validated molecular biomarker; CrisPRO could develop predictive transcriptional or epigenetic signatures to enrich future pediatric trials.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "We didn't ever develop a biomarker for it. So it was really just a reduction in the blast count was a true indicator of the on-target activity."
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: Use CrisPRO's computational spine to integrate RNA‑seq and ChIP‑seq data from menin‑dependent leukemias to derive a robust on‑target biomarker panel.

crispro_opportunity.description: No resistance mechanisms were described; CrisPRO can model potential escape pathways (e.g., alternative HOX/MEIS activation) to guide combination strategies.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker noted: "We saw a single agent effect in 7 of 8 pediatric PDXs, but did not discuss mechanisms of non‑responders."
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: Apply CrisPRO's network analysis to predict secondary targets that may mediate resistance and prioritize rational combos.

unresolved_questions: What molecular biomarkers could predict response to menin inhibition beyond blast count?
What are the mechanisms of primary or acquired resistance in the non‑responding PDX models?

talk_metadata.session_title: Advancing Early Phase Pediatric Clinical Trials to Turn Promising Agents into Actionable Evidence
talk_metadata.talk_title: Regulatory Considerations for Early‑Phase Pediatric Oncology Trials
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "The FDA generally requests that sponsors propose a reasonable minimum number of patients to study in clinical trials in each relevant age group."

Speaker said: "We are willing to apply a reasonable approach and do not expect large single‑arm cohorts of each agent in the combination, particularly if it's a combination that's been used as part of standard of care."

key_data_gaps: No specific trial data presented; discussion focused on regulatory frameworks

crispro_opportunity.description: FDA highlighted flexibility for adaptive, seamless designs; CrisPRO can provide computational modeling to optimize dose‑escalation and cohort allocation for rare pediatric populations.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "Seamless designs, as was in the trametinib dabrafenib example, that allow for adequate toxicity PK data and comparisons for efficacy"
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: Leverage CrisPRO's simulation engine to predict optimal adaptive trial pathways and reduce patient numbers while maintaining statistical power.

crispro_opportunity.description: Regulatory guidance emphasizes need for age‑appropriate biomarkers; CrisPRO can generate in‑silico biomarker panels to satisfy FDA expectations early in IND filings.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "We need to think about PK and age associated toxicity as well. Impact of trial design..."
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: Integrate pharmacokinetic modeling with pediatric physiology to propose biomarker‑guided dosing strategies.

unresolved_questions: How will the FDA evaluate novel adaptive designs that combine dose‑finding and expansion cohorts in ultra‑rare pediatric cancers?
What level of biomarker evidence is sufficient to waive enrollment of certain age sub‑groups?
